metadata.title: Standard Deviations: Flawed Assumptions, Tortured Data — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown
metadata.credit_line: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. We remember remarkable results and forget the mundane, biasing what we treat as evidence. A key error is assuming computers and their outputs are infallible. With a small base, statistical flukes get amplified and masquerade as meaningful patterns. Superficial comparisons, like $150 million of corporate profit sold as local benefit, hide the real figure.

artifactLines: We remember remarkable results and forget the mundane, biasing what we treat as evidence.
A key error is assuming computers and their outputs are infallible.
With a small base, statistical flukes get amplified and masquerade as meaningful patterns.
Superficial comparisons, like $150 million of corporate profit sold as local benefit, hide the real figure.

beats.narration_text: The line that stuck with me is that we remember remarkable results and forget the mundane, so a statistical fluke on a small base can look like signal. I also liked exposing the $150 million figure as corporate profit dressed up as local benefit. Take a below-freezing-launch-day style claim and (1) show how a small base amplifies a fluke, (2) separate the remarkable outlier worth studying from noise, and (3) unmask one superficial comparison hiding what the number really measures.
